source_paper_title: Minimization Fractional Prophet Inequalities for Sequential Procurement

Page 6, end of Section 1.3 (Our results) and reiterated on page 18, end of Section 4.2 (More than two sellers)

This helps give some intuition as to why computing the precise competitive ratio for all \(n \ge 2, p > 1\) is a challenging task. We leave it as an open question to analytically resolve the competitive ratio.

Computing the exact competitive ratio as a function of the problem parameters \((p, n, \ell, u)\) is a challenging problem.
title: Compute competitive ratio for fractional minimization prophet inequalities without in-house production
background_latex:
Fix integers \(n\ge 2\) and a real exponent \(p>1\). Let \(\ell,u\) satisfy \(0<\ell\le u<\infty\). For each stage \(i\in\{1,\dots,n\}\), a cost coefficient \(C_i\) is drawn independently from an (a priori known) distribution \(D_i\) supported on \([\ell,u]\). After observing \(C_i=c_i\), an online algorithm chooses a purchase amount \(x_i\in[0,1]\), where \(x_i\) may depend on \((c_1,\dots,c_i)\), and must satisfy the ex post feasibility constraint \(\sum_{i=1}^n x_i = 1\). The realized procurement cost under exponent \(p\) is
\[
\mathrm{ALG}_\pi(c,p)=\sum_{i=1}^n c_i x_i^p,
\]
where \(\pi=(\pi_1,\dots,\pi_n)\) denotes the online policy and \(x_i=\pi_i(c_1,\dots,c_i)\).

For a realized vector \(c=(c_1,\dots,c_n)\), define the clairvoyant (prophet) benchmark as the optimal value of the offline convex program
\[
\mathrm{OPT}(c,p)=\min_{x\in[0,1]^n}\ \sum_{i=1}^n c_i x_i^p\quad\text{s.t.}\quad \sum_{i=1}^n x_i=1.
\]
The competitive ratio for the family \(\mathcal D\) of allowable distributions is
\[
\mathrm{CR}(\mathcal D,p,n)=\inf_{\pi}\ \sup_{(D_1,\dots,D_n)\in\mathcal D^n}\ \frac{\mathbb E[\mathrm{ALG}_\pi(C,p)]}{\mathbb E[\mathrm{OPT}(C,p)]},
\]
where the expectation is over \(C_i\sim D_i\) independently.

In the "without in-house production" setting, there is no additional deterministic option \(C_1\equiv 1\); all \(n\) stages correspond to random sellers, and the supremum ranges over \(D_1,\dots,D_n\) in the allowed class (e.g., supported on \([\ell,u]\)).
problem_definition_latex:
Determine (in closed form, as a function of \(p,n,\ell,u\)) the competitive ratio
\[
\mathrm{CR}(\mathcal D,p,n)=\inf_{\pi}\ \sup_{D_1,\dots,D_n\ \text{supported on }[\ell,u]}\ \frac{\mathbb E[\mathrm{ALG}_\pi(C,p)]}{\mathbb E[\mathrm{OPT}(C,p)]}
\]
for the fractional minimization prophet inequality without in-house production (i.e., with \(n\) random sellers only), beyond the special case \(p=2,n=2\) treated explicitly in the paper.

location_in_paper: Page 6, end of Section 1.3 (Our results) and reiterated on page 18, end of Section 4.2 (More than two sellers)
open_problem_quote:
This helps give some intuition as to why computing the precise competitive ratio for all \(n \ge 2, p > 1\) is a challenging task. We leave it as an open question to analytically resolve the competitive ratio.

Computing the exact competitive ratio as a function of the problem parameters \((p, n, \ell, u)\) is a challenging problem.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: For fixed \((p,n,\ell,u)\) and a discretized support, the worst-case distribution search becomes a large but finitely parameterized nonconvex optimization that can be brute-forced or locally optimized, potentially revealing structure or candidate extremal distributions. However, the true problem is infinite-dimensional over distributions, so computational searches can suggest conjectures but not decisively refute/confirm the exact formula without additional theory.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: Candidate policies \(\pi\) (e.g., parameterized thresholding/dynamic-programming approximations) and candidate hard instances (parameterized discrete distributions on \([\ell,u]\)) can both be represented in code, with an automatic fitness signal given by Monte Carlo estimation of \(\mathbb E[\mathrm{ALG}_\pi]/\mathbb E[\mathrm{OPT}]\) and inner optimization over \(x\) for \(\mathrm{OPT}\). An evolutionary loop could search jointly over policy families and adversarial distributions to conjecture sharp bounds or identify extremal distribution structures for given \((p,n,\ell,u)\).

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The open problem asks for a closed-form characterization of the optimal worst-case competitive ratio for the fractional minimization prophet inequality without in-house production, i.e., with only random sellers and convex monomial costs \(\sum_i c_i x_i^p\) under the ex post constraint \(\sum_i x_i=1\). The source paper resolves the case \(p=2,n=2\) (Theorem 3) by reducing the ratio \(\mathbb E[\mathrm{ALG}]/\mathbb E[\mathrm{OPT}]\) to an infinite-dimensional optimization over distributions and then showing the worst case is attained by a point mass at \(\sqrt{\ell u}\) for one seller and a two-point (\(\ell,u\)) distribution for the other, yielding \(\tfrac14(\sqrt{u/\ell}+\sqrt{\ell/u})+\tfrac12\). It also shows that for \(p=2\) the worst case over \(n\) is not achieved at \(n=2\) and that the competitive ratio can be non-monotone in \(n\), highlighting why extending Theorem 3 to general \(n\) and \(p\) is difficult.

Among the provided forward citations, only "Online Contract Selection for Continual Coverage" is available and it does not directly advance the sequential procurement model. Its main relevance is methodological: it develops quantile-space LP/duality techniques and obtains exact worst-case competitive ratios via recurrence/ODE characterizations in a different minimization online setting. Such tools suggest a possible direction for the open problem: recasting the sequential procurement policy design and worst-case distribution search in an appropriate functional/dual space (e.g., via transformations using Lemma 1’s power-mean form \(\mathrm{OPT}(c,p)=(\sum_i c_i^q)^{1/q}\) with \(q=-1/(p-1)\)) to derive extremal distributions and potentially a characterization (even if not a simple closed form) of \(\mathrm{CR}(\mathcal D,p,n)\) for bounded supports \([\ell,u]\). However, no cited work currently provides such a characterization for the procurement problem without in-house production beyond the \(p=2,n=2\) case, so the problem remains open.

sources.title: Online Contract Selection for Continual Coverage
sources.source_url: (source URL not provided in forward-citation analysis)
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper studies online contract selection with continual coverage under i.i.d. prices. For the deferred-contract model (OSDC), it gives an exact characterization of the optimal worst-case competitive ratio: for each horizon length \(n\), the optimal ratio equals \(\zeta_n\) where \(\zeta_n\) is the unique value for which a recurrence system (Eq. (3) in the paper) admits a decreasing solution \(0\le \varepsilon_n<\cdots<\varepsilon_1=1\); moreover \(\zeta_n = n/(\varepsilon_n P_n'(1-\varepsilon_n)+P_n(1-\varepsilon_n))\). As \(n\to\infty\), \(\zeta_n\to \zeta^*\approx 2.472\), characterized as the unique parameter for which an ODE boundary-value problem (Eqs. (1)–(2)) has a solution. For the concurrent-contract model (OSCC), it gives improved asymptotic bounds, including \(\limsup_{n\to\infty} R_n^C\le 4.179\), and shows no constant competitive ratio is possible under independent non-i.i.d. prices.
sources.connection: The open problem asks for the closed-form optimal competitive ratio for a different model: fractional minimization prophet inequalities for sequential procurement with convex cost \(\sum_i c_i x_i^p\) and ex post constraint \(\sum_i x_i=1\), without an in-house deterministic option, for arbitrary \(p>1\), \(n\), and support \([\ell,u]\). The citing paper does not study fractional procurement, does not have the \(x_i^p\) convexity structure, and its objective is linear in contract length (cost \(T_i X_i\)) with a temporal coverage constraint. Thus it neither computes nor bounds \(\mathrm{CR}(\mathcal D,p,n)\) for the fractional procurement problem. The only connection is methodological/background: it uses quantile-based algorithms and primal–dual/LP-in-quantile-space techniques for minimization-style prophet/online selection problems, and it highlights impossibility phenomena in non-i.i.d. settings, which may be conceptually relevant when considering distributional assumptions in the procurement prophet-inequality setting.
